serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 200 ml serving of low fat milk, equivalent to approximately 206 g, as commonly sold in Singapore cafes and fast-food outlets such as Dunkin' Donuts.

nutrient_source.title: Milk, lowfat, fluid, 1% milkfat, with added vitamin A and vitamin D (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 172179)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for 1% low fat fluid milk used as the primary nutrient reference. Values are per 100 g of fluid milk. This is the closest approved-source match for a generic low fat milk product sold in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Milk, full fat and low fat entries; confirmed in University of Sydney GI Database (milk, low fat)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Low fat milk has a well-established GI of approximately 32 (range 27–37) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table. GI = 32 used. GL per 200 ml serving calculated as: (32 × 4.99 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 206 g serving) / 100 = approximately 3.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (44 mg per 100 g); beneficial potassium and calcium
hypertension.guidance: Low fat milk is appropriate for individuals with hypertension; its potassium (150 mg/100 g) and calcium (125 mg/100 g) content may support blood pressure regulation, and its sodium level (44 mg/100 g) is modest within a 200 ml serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 32) and moderate lactose content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Low fat milk has a low glycemic index of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.3 per 200 ml serving, making it a suitable dairy choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat and saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: With only 0.97 g fat and 0.63 g saturated fat per 100 g, low fat milk is a heart-friendly dairy option that provides calcium and B vitamins without the saturated fat burden of whole milk, consistent with cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low fat milk is pasteurized cow's milk from which most of the fat has been removed, typically retaining approximately 1% milkfat. It is a widely consumed dairy beverage sold in 200 ml cartons or cups at cafes, fast-food outlets, and convenience stores across Singapore, including chains such as Dunkin' Donuts. The flavour is mildly sweet and creamy, lighter in body than whole milk but richer than skim milk. Nutritionally, a 200 ml serving provides approximately 86 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 2.0 g fat, and 10.3 g carbohydrate, along with significant calcium (approximately 258 mg), potassium, vitamin B12, riboflavin, and added vitamins A and D. Its glycemic index is low (GI ≈ 32), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Low fat milk is appropriate for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed in standard portions. It is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains the milk allergen and is not suitable for those with lactose intolerance or dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in low fat milk 200ml?
Is low fat milk good for diabetics?
Nutrition facts low fat milk Singapore
Low fat milk vs whole milk calories
Dunkin Donuts low fat milk nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for 1% low fat fluid milk (FDC ID 172179), an approved primary source. GI value of 32 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Ontology codes are verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: Serving size set to 206 g based on 200 ml volume with a density of approximately 1.03 g/ml for low fat milk. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (naturally zero by product formulation for this grade of milk). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as milk contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; milk is not a source of vitamin C. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 10.28) / 100 ≈ 3.3. The input record listed cuisine as 'Coffee' reflecting the field survey platform category (Dunkin' Donuts); the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western / Global' as low fat milk is a generic dairy product. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 sentinel not used; actual value 0.28 g sourced from USDA. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported or not applicable: none remain as -1 in this record since USDA SR Legacy provides comprehensive coverage for this food.

Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for trans_fat_g, dietary_fibre_g, and vitamin_c_mg.
